search_terms_used: iron deficiency running performance systematic review meta-analysis
vitamin D bone health runners systematic review
footstrike haemolysis runners intravascular hemolysis distance running
calcium bone density running stress fractures endurance athletes
haematological adaptations endurance training sports anemia runners
coverage_notes: Strong coverage of iron deficiency/supplementation in athletes (4 papers including meta-analyses and systematic reviews), footstrike haemolysis (2 papers), vitamin D and bone health (3 papers), bone health broadly (1 paper), sports anemia (1 paper), and RED-S as context for bone/micronutrient health (1 paper). Populations span endurance athletes, runners, military recruits, and mixed sport athletes. Slight gap in masters/youth-specific running populations. Good mix of study types: 2 meta-analyses, 3 systematic/scoping reviews, 1 RCT, 2 narrative reviews, 2 review articles, 1 consensus statement, 1 experimental study.

papers.title: Is iron treatment beneficial in, iron-deficient but non-anaemic (IDNA) endurance athletes? A systematic review and meta-analysis
papers.notes: Meta-analysis examining whether iron supplementation improves VO2max and exercise performance in iron-deficient but non-anaemic endurance athletes.

papers.title: Effects of Oral Iron Supplementation on Blood Iron Status in Athletes: A Systematic Review, Meta-Analysis and Meta-Regression of Randomized Controlled Trials
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 13 RCTs examining effects of oral iron supplementation on blood iron parameters and physical performance in healthy adult athletes.

papers.title: Does Iron Supplementation Improve Performance in Iron-Deficient Nonanemic Athletes?
papers.notes: Systematic review evaluating whether iron supplementation improves athletic performance in iron-deficient nonanemic athletes.

papers.title: Iron deficiency in sports - definition, influence on performance and therapy
papers.notes: Swiss Society of Sports Medicine consensus statement providing diagnostic criteria and treatment guidelines for iron deficiency in athletes.

papers.title: Iron Status and Physical Performance in Athletes
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of iron metabolism, prevalence of iron deficiency in athletes, and the relationship between iron status and physical performance.

papers.title: Iron considerations for the athlete: a narrative review
papers.notes: Narrative review examining iron absorption, sex-based differences, training and altitude effects on iron status, and supplementation strategies for athletes.

papers.title: Athletic induced iron deficiency: new insights into the role of inflammation, cytokines and hormones
papers.notes: Review examining the mechanisms of exercise-induced iron deficiency including the roles of inflammation, hepcidin, cytokines, and hormones in athletes.

papers.title: Footstrike is the major cause of hemolysis during running
papers.notes: Experimental study comparing plasma free hemoglobin after uphill vs downhill treadmill running to isolate footstrike as the primary mechanism of exercise-induced hemolysis.

papers.title: Foot-strike Hemolysis: A Scoping Review of Long-Distance Runners
papers.notes: Scoping review synthesizing evidence on foot-strike hemolysis prevalence, mechanisms, and clinical significance in long-distance runners.

papers.title: Bone Health in Athletes: The Role of Exercise, Nutrition, and Hormones
papers.notes: Review examining the interplay of exercise type, calcium, vitamin D, energy availability, and hormonal status on bone mineral density and stress fracture risk in athletes.

papers.title: Vitamin D and Stress Fractures in Sport: Preventive and Therapeutic Measures-A Narrative Review
papers.notes: Narrative review examining the relationship between vitamin D status and stress fracture incidence in athletes, with preventive and therapeutic recommendations.

papers.title: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation decreases incidence of stress fractures in female navy recruits
papers.journal: Journal of Bone and Mineral Research
papers.notes: Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial showing 2000mg calcium plus 800IU vitamin D daily reduced stress fracture incidence by 20% in female navy recruits during basic training.
